frameworks.name: resting-state functional connectivity
frameworks.one_line: Pre-stimulation network connectivity as predictor of individual tDCS response.

frameworks.name: default mode network / central executive network
frameworks.one_line: Two WM-relevant networks indexed for connectivity with stimulation site.

frameworks.name: transcranial direct current stimulation
frameworks.one_line: Anodal right frontal tDCS paired with visual WM training.

core_question: Can resting-state connectivity between the tDCS stimulation site and WM-relevant networks predict initial and longitudinal benefits from tDCS-paired WM training?
key_assumptions: individual differences drive variable tDCS outcomes
DMN and CEN are the WM-relevant networks for right frontal stimulation
resting-state connectivity is a stable individual trait
manipulations: active anodal right frontal tDCS vs sham
resting-state connectivity measured between stimulation site and DMN/CEN
key_findings: only the active tDCS group significantly improved over the training period
no significant correlation between initial tDCS response and resting-state connectivity
DMN activity in anterior cingulate cortex correlated with WM training slope
interpretations: individual differences in baseline network activity shape longitudinal tDCS-paired training gains
sampling considerations matter critically in tDCS studies
limitations_noted: single-session tDCS designs are prone to variable outcomes
